findings.acceptance_test: The emotion is legible through Paul's behavior, physical sensation, or character-specific vocabulary before any abstract label appears. If 'jealousy' or 'indignation' still appear, they arrive after the reader has already felt the feeling through specific detail.
findings.evidence: Line 3235: 'What arrived was not grief. Grief he could have handled... What arrived instead was flatter and more humiliating: jealousy. His best friend of twenty-five years had been dead four days, and Paul’s first response to the will was the petty, undeniable indignation of a man who had been assuming, for a quarter century, that he was Martin’s person.'
findings.problem: Paul's emotional response to the will is introduced through abstract emotion-naming ('jealousy,' 'indignation') rather than embodied sensation. The style bible profiles Paul as using concrete, risk-assessment vocabulary internally ('exposure,' 'adjust,' 'total loss'). While the passage recovers with 250 lines of specific memories and observations that embody the feeling, the entry point names the emotion before rendering it, inverting the prose's own principle of showing before telling. Additionally, the opening construction ('What arrived was not grief... What arrived instead') uses the P-03 corrective template, compounding two issues in a single passage.
findings.rewrite_direction: Open with the physical or behavioral manifestation of the jealousy — Paul's hands on the armrest, his re-reading of the specific line naming Tommy, the involuntary mental inventory of his own contributions — before any abstract label appears. The insurance vocabulary could do the work: Paul feeling 'adjusted down' or registering 'total loss' in the language his brain naturally reaches for. If 'jealousy' must appear, let it arrive after at least two sentences of embodied rendering, as discovery rather than announcement.

chapter_hits.acceptance_test: Tommy's dialogue uses contracted forms in this exchange. Paul's single uncontracted form is acceptable only if no other uncontracted Paul lines appear within 500 lines of text.
chapter_hits.evidence: Line 3993: Paul says 'I am hearing it' (uncontracted 'I am' where 'I'm' would be natural for moderate contraction level). Line 3995: Tommy replies 'No, you're hearing the words. You are not hearing the thing' — the second clause uses uncontracted 'You are not,' violating Tommy's high contraction level and short-declarative syntax signature.
chapter_hits.problem: This exchange establishes the uncontracted-for-emphasis pattern that escalates across later chapters. Tommy's 'You are not' is the more egregious violation — his style bible profile specifies high contraction level and short declaratives. The formal diction reads as narratorial rather than as Tommy's working-class coaching voice.
chapter_hits.rewrite_direction: Paul's 'I am hearing it' could stand as a single emphatic beat if isolated. Tommy's reply should contract: 'You're not hearing the thing' — which is more threatening in Tommy's clipped register and consistent with his profile. Alternatively, Tommy's version could fragment: 'No. You're hearing the words. Not the thing.' — using the sentence fragmentation his stress_tells describe.

chapter_hits.acceptance_test: Paul's dialogue in Chapter 16 uses contracted forms throughout except where the text explicitly frames an uncontracted form as a deliberate register shift. The 5906 speech must read as spoken under emotional pressure: contracted, possibly syntactically imperfect, with no more than one balanced aphorism (not two in parallel). Joel's responses use variable contractions per his profile, defaulting to contracted.
chapter_hits.evidence: Lines 5672-5678: Paul says 'we do not bumble around' and 'you do not start lawyering her.' Joel responds 'That is a deeply unfair condition.' Three consecutive uncontracted forms across two speakers. Line 5902: Paul says 'Do not tell me what Martin made.' Line 5906: Paul delivers the triple anaphora — 'I am not talking about helping Tommy run. I am not talking about hiding the paper. I am not talking about saving him from Mallick. I'm talking about not letting the whole thing come down on him...' followed by 'Martin turned him into a long-term project. Cliff turned him into leverage. I want one hour where he's just a man sitting in the wreckage he made.'
chapter_hits.problem: Chapter 16 contains 6 uncontracted dialogue forms from Paul alone, plus Joel's 'That is.' The triple anaphora at 5906 is the novel's most critically undermined moment: Paul's emotional case for confronting Tommy should be raw and pressured, but the rhetorical structure (three parallel 'I am not' clauses pivoting to one 'I'm' clause, followed by two perfectly balanced aphorisms about Martin and Cliff) reads as a pre-composed speech. The aphorisms ('turned him into a long-term project' / 'turned him into leverage') have identical syntactic weight, landing as literary compression rather than a man finding words under grief. The style bible says Paul under stress gets 'more precise, not less' and 'sentences tighten' — but tightened precision is not the same as formal rhetoric.
chapter_hits.rewrite_direction: Contract all forms: 'we don't bumble around,' 'you don't start lawyering her,' Joel's 'That's a deeply unfair condition.' For the 5906 speech: rebuild with contractions throughout. 'I'm not talking about helping Tommy run. I'm not talking about hiding the paper.' The anaphoric structure survives contraction — the emotional repetition is in the content, not the diction. For the aphorisms: consider breaking the parallel ('Martin made him a project for twenty-two years. And Cliff — Cliff just saw a handle.') — asymmetry reads as thought-in-motion rather than pre-composed rhetoric.

chapter_hits.acceptance_test: Paul's speeches at 6575 and 6627 use contracted forms. No antithetical balance between sentences (the giving/that-is-all/giving mirror structure is dissolved). The speeches read as a man making a decision under pressure, not as prepared rhetoric.
chapter_hits.evidence: Line 6575: 'Meaning I am not spending the next twenty years adjusting around what you did. I am not taking your keys and making calls and helping you keep a lid on it and pretending that's friendship. Martin made his choice. I'm making mine.' Line 6627: 'I am giving you the night. That is all I am giving you.'
chapter_hits.problem: Same pattern as Chapter 16: anaphoric uncontracted negation ('I am not... I am not...') followed by a contracted pivot ('I'm making mine'). At 6627, the antithetical structure ('I am giving you X / That is all I am giving you') is balanced to the syllable — this is compositional craft, not speech. Paul is giving Tommy the most consequential ultimatum of the novel, and the diction places the reader in a courtroom rather than on a deck between old friends. The contracted 'I'm' buried between the uncontracted 'I am' clauses at 6575 makes the formality choice inconsistent rather than emphatic.
chapter_hits.rewrite_direction: At 6575: 'I'm not spending the next twenty years adjusting around what you did. I'm not taking your keys and making calls and keeping a lid on it and calling that friendship.' — contracts throughout, replaces 'pretending that's' with 'calling that' for harder landing. At 6627: 'I'm giving you tonight. That's all I've got.' — shorter, harder, more consistent with Paul's tightened-under-stress profile. The ultimatum's force should come from brevity and finality, not from formal diction.

pattern_findings.global_problem: Paul Decker's dialogue defaults to uncontracted rhetorical anaphora ('I am not talking about,' 'I am not spending,' 'I am giving you') at every major emotional speech in the novel's second half. This creates a workshopped quality — prepared monologues rather than spontaneous speech under pressure. The style bible specifies Paul's contraction_level as 'moderate' and his stress_tells as 'sentences tighten and lose their qualifiers.' The uncontracted anaphoric pattern does the opposite: it inflates and formalizes under stress. At lines 5906, 6575, and 6627 — Paul's three most consequential speeches — the reader hears a character performing a closing argument rather than breaking open. This is the novel's most damaging single craft issue because it undermines the emotional authority of the climactic Paul-Tommy confrontation across two chapters.

chapter_hits.acceptance_test: Both instances remain. They should feel like sensory discovery, not the first two entries in a count.
chapter_hits.evidence: Line 309: 'The refrigerator cycled on somewhere above him.' Line 513: 'The refrigerator hummed somewhere below him.'
chapter_hits.problem: Two instances in the opening chapter. Both work atmospherically — the first marks the quiet after friends disperse, the second accompanies Nate's discovery of Martin. The spatial inversion (above/below) does genuine character work. These are the strongest early uses and should be preserved.
chapter_hits.rewrite_direction: Keep both. These establish the motif and ground the house as a body with mechanical organs. They set the reader's ear for the sound without yet feeling repetitive.

chapter_hits.acceptance_test: Retained. The three-verb construction remains unique to this chapter.
chapter_hits.evidence: Line 2323: 'Above his head the refrigerator kicked on, rattled through its cycle, clicked off.'
chapter_hits.problem: Strong instance that does genuine character work — Doug lying awake with his hidden medications, the cycle marking sleeplessness. The three-verb sequence (kicked, rattled, clicked) mirrors his restless waiting. This earns its place.
chapter_hits.rewrite_direction: Retain. This is the motif at its most purposeful — the mechanical cycle as a clock for Doug's insomnia and shame.

chapter_hits.acceptance_test: Chapter 18 contains no more than two refrigerator references. No descriptor ('arthritic,' 'grinding,' 'forty-minute') appears in two consecutive chapters. The total manuscript count of active-verb refrigerator instances is reduced to 8-10.
chapter_hits.evidence: Lines 6754, 6816, 6966, 7212: Four instances in the final chapter alone. 'The refrigerator worked through its arthritic cycle' (6754), 'The refrigerator clicked off' (6816), 'The refrigerator kicked back on' (6966), 'the refrigerator clicked off' (7212).
chapter_hits.problem: Four instances in one chapter is mechanical tracking, not atmosphere. Lines 6816 and 7212 are near-identical ('clicked off'). The chapter becomes a cycle counter — each fridge mention is a clock tick rather than an environmental detail. The 'arthritic cycle' descriptor at 6754 duplicates 6047's 'arthritic cycles,' creating a repeated metaphor across consecutive chapters.
chapter_hits.rewrite_direction: Reduce to two instances: keep 6754 (Mallick setting up in the kitchen, the fridge as witness) and 6966 (the fridge filling the silence during Tommy's confession — load-bearing). Cut 6816 and 7212 entirely. For 6754, change 'arthritic' to a different descriptor to avoid echoing Ch 17's identical word.

pattern_findings.global_problem: The refrigerator-cycling motif appears 17 times with active verbs (kicked, cycled, hummed, clicked, rattled, worked) across 12 of 18 chapters. While established in the continuity sheet as an environmental constant and genuinely effective in early chapters as atmospheric grounding, the repetition crosses from intentional motif into mechanical habit by mid-book. The constitution's threshold — 'if a descriptive default appears more than four times in the book, it is a tic, not a style' — is exceeded fourfold. A reader tracking the pattern will begin anticipating the next cycle mention, which converts atmospheric texture into structural furniture. Additionally, descriptor repetition compounds the issue: 'arthritic cycle' appears at both line 6047 (Ch 17) and line 6754 (Ch 18); 'forty-minute' appears at lines 606, 994, 1457, and 4911.

chapter_hits.acceptance_test: No more than one 'not X but Y' corrective construction per scene. Where two appear within 50 lines, one is rephrased as a positive assertion or a different syntactic structure.
chapter_hits.evidence: Line 546: 'not the cinematic crack of breaking ribs but a soft wet yielding.' Line 596: 'not to save Martin but to not be the person who stopped trying.'
chapter_hits.problem: Two corrective constructions in the CPR scene within 50 lines. Line 546 subverts the movie expectation of CPR — this is load-bearing and should stay. Line 596 articulates Paul's motive with honest precision but uses the same template, making the narrator's clarification habit visible when two instances appear this close.
chapter_hits.rewrite_direction: Keep 546 (the physical subversion is essential). Revise 596 to a positive statement: 'He kept going because stopping would turn him into the person who stopped' or 'He kept going because the alternative was being the one who quit.' Remove the negation frame while preserving the self-knowledge.

chapter_hits.acceptance_test: Tommy's POV chapters contain no 'not X but Y' corrective constructions. His interior narration uses simple declaratives and physical/spatial metaphors as specified in the style bible profile.
chapter_hits.evidence: Line 3360: 'not because the beer mattered but because empty hands felt worse.' Line 3538 (approximate): 'not like a barrel fire or a pit fire but like something finally getting fed.'
chapter_hits.problem: Two instances in Tommy's POV chapter. Tommy's style bible profile specifies 'short declarative sentences' and 'avoids abstract language — reaches for physical and spatial metaphors.' The corrective construction is syntactically complex (subordinate clause with negation and comparison) and reads as narratorial rather than Tommy's working-class consciousness.
chapter_hits.rewrite_direction: For 3360: 'He needed something in his hands. The beer was closest.' — Tommy's short declaratives, physical motivation. For 3538: 'It ate like something hungry, like something that had been waiting.' — removes the comparative negation, stays in Tommy's concrete-image vocabulary.

chapter_hits.acceptance_test: Chapter 14's body-discovery scene uses direct sensory language. The corrective construction appears no more than once in the chapter, and not in the first 50 lines of the discovery.
chapter_hits.evidence: Line 4668: 'not the give of sleeping muscle but the stiffness of flesh.' Line 4724: 'not to understand but to contain.'
chapter_hits.problem: Two instances in the chapter where Doug discovers Cliff's body — a high-pressure moment where the corrective template should yield to immediate sensory impact. The negation-then-correction adds a processing step ('this is not A, it is B') where the character should be registering sensation directly, without the comparator.
chapter_hits.rewrite_direction: For 4668: 'The stiffness of flesh that had stopped being a person's.' — direct sensation, no negation. For 4724: 'He was trying to contain it, not to understand it.' — if the corrective is retained, invert the order so the primary action (containing) comes first and the negation is afterthought.

pattern_findings.global_problem: The 'not X but Y' corrective construction — in forms including 'not [A] but [B],' 'not because [A] but because [B],' 'not [A] — [B],' and 'not [A], not [B]' — appears 17+ times across 10 chapters. The construction functions as the narrator's default precision tool: clarifying what something is by first stating what it is not. While individually effective (each instance calibrates an observation), in aggregate it becomes the prose's dominant clarification habit, producing a predictable rhythm where every nuanced moment arrives through negation-then-correction. The constitution's threshold of 'more than four times in the book' is exceeded fourfold. The pattern spans multiple POV characters, confirming it as a narrator-level tic rather than character-specific interiority.

chapter_hits.acceptance_test: Each loon instance after Ch 2 interacts with the POV character's current state or the scene's specific pressure. No two loon sentences share the same syntactic template.
chapter_hits.evidence: Line 1218: 'Outside, a loon called from the lake, that wavering sound carrying across the water and in through the screen door.'
chapter_hits.problem: By the fourth instance, 'a loon called from' has become predictable. The added descriptor ('wavering sound carrying across the water') attempts variation but uses the same sentence architecture as Ch 2's extended loon description. The sound doesn't interact with the scene or the POV character.
chapter_hits.rewrite_direction: Filter through the POV character's state: what does the loon sound like to Joel, who is sitting in a room where Mallick is interviewing his friends about his dead friend? The sound could interrupt a thought, or be noted and dismissed, or be mistaken for something else. The loon should arrive as character experience, not as narrative wallpaper.

chapter_hits.acceptance_test: Chapter 18's loon instances are retained. Total manuscript count is reduced from 10 to 6-7 by cutting the most generic mid-novel instances (Ch 4, Ch 7, Ch 11) or replacing them with varied sensory details.
chapter_hits.evidence: Line 6894: 'Outside, a loon called once across the water. The note hung there and went flat.' Line 7210: 'A loon called again, farther out this time.'
chapter_hits.problem: These two closing instances are the strongest late-novel uses — 6894's 'hung there and went flat' carries emotional weight (flatness as post-confession exhaustion), and 7210's 'farther out' suggests recession/departure. Both work. The issue is cumulative: by instance 9 and 10, even good instances carry the weight of the 7 generic ones before them.
chapter_hits.rewrite_direction: Retain both Ch 18 instances. The cumulative-fatigue problem is solved by improving instances 3-8, not by cutting the bookend pair. If the mid-novel instances are varied and POV-filtered, these final two will land with the weight they deserve.

pattern_findings.global_problem: The loon-call motif appears 10 times across 10 chapters (1, 2, 4, 7, 8, 10, 11, 12, 18), with 7 instances using the near-identical template 'A/a loon called from [location].' The motif's structural absence in Chapters 14-17 (investigation/confrontation) and return in Chapter 18 (resolution) is a sound design choice — the lake's natural voice withdrawing during the human crisis and returning after. However, the verbal template is too consistent: the reader begins completing the sentence pattern by Ch 8. The three instances that vary the template — line 606 ('that sliding, deranged sound'), line 2964 (sound hanging until Mallick breaks it), and line 6894 ('The note hung there and went flat') — demonstrate how the motif works when the loon interacts with character or narrative rather than simply being noted.

chapter_hits.acceptance_test: No chapter contains more than one 'the kind of [noun] that [clause]' construction. Total manuscript count is reduced to 4 or fewer, with surviving instances in different POV chapters.
chapter_hits.evidence: Line 1806: Two instances on a single line — 'the kind of source a serious person would not consult' and 'the sort of credential a courtroom respected.' Both appear in Nate's POV narration.
chapter_hits.problem: Two instances of the template on a single line makes the construction's mechanical nature unmissable. The doubled use in Nate's chapter is particularly conspicuous because Nate's style bible profile emphasizes 'long-winded and list-building' syntax, not type-calibration syntax.
chapter_hits.rewrite_direction: Keep one, rephrase the other. For the first: 'a source no serious person would consult' — removes the template while preserving the judgment. For the second: rephrase directly — 'credentials that held up in court.' Nate's voice should build lists and riff, not calibrate types.

chapter_hits.acceptance_test: Line 3297's 'the kind of thing' formulation is replaced with a specific, character-voiced observation. No two instances of the construction appear within 100 lines.
chapter_hits.evidence: Line 3235: 'the kind of feeling that should have made him quieter.' Line 3297: 'the kind of thing that happened in long-running friend groups.'
chapter_hits.problem: Two instances within 62 lines, both in Paul's POV. Line 3297 is the weaker instance — 'the kind of thing that happened in long-running friend groups' is vague and generic, the sort of observation that could appear in any novel about any group of people. It lacks the specificity the constitution demands.
chapter_hits.rewrite_direction: Cut 3297 entirely or replace with a Paul-specific observation: a comparison to claims he's adjusted, a specific memory of the group absorbing a previous shock. Line 3235 can stand if P-03's correction removes the 'not X but Y' frame that wraps it — without the corrective scaffolding, the type-calibration is less visible.

pattern_findings.global_problem: The construction 'the kind of [noun] that [clause]' appears 8 times across the novel (lines approximately 897, 1631, 1806, 1806, 2384, 2699, 3235, 3297), exceeding the constitution's 4-per-book tic threshold. While individually effective — each instance calibrates an abstraction through a concrete relative clause — the pattern becomes a recognizable prose move: the narrator's way of being specific about type. The construction appears across multiple POV characters (Joel, Mallick, Nate, Cliff, Paul), confirming it as narrator-level rather than character-specific.

summary: Keeper League demonstrates genuine shortlist-level strengths: the comic register in Chapters 1-13 is sustained, character-specific, and rhythmically controlled; the tonal pivot at Chapter 14 lands as a gear change rather than a dimming; Mallick's dual-register voice is the manuscript's standout craft achievement; the fire flashback (Ch 12) and dock-murder confession (Ch 18) are the strongest sustained writing in the manuscript, delivering embodied specificity that meets the constitution's rawness standard without flinching. Profanity register (308 instances), sensory violence, and moral discomfort are fully present — this novel is not sanitized. Continuity is clean across all verified axes (body position, safe location, RT document chain, timeline, will contents, character ages). The principal failure modes, in order of consequence: (1) Paul Decker's dialogue defaults to uncontracted rhetorical anaphora at every emotional climax in Chapters 13, 16, and 17, producing speeches that read as workshopped closing arguments rather than spontaneous human speech at the precise moments where the novel needs maximum authenticity; (2) the refrigerator-cycling motif appears 17 times with active verbs across 12 chapters, crossing from atmospheric touchstone into mechanical repetition that a reader will consciously track by mid-book; (3) the 'not X but Y' corrective construction appears 17+ times across 10 chapters, registering as the narrator's default clarification habit rather than a controlled stylistic choice. Two additional low-severity pattern tics (loon calls in near-identical phrasing 10 times across 10 chapters; 'the kind of X that Y' construction 8 times) compound the repetition issue. None of these findings are structural — the plot architecture, character arcs, tonal management, and rawness calibration are sound. All three MEDIUM findings are addressable in a targeted revision pass without disrupting the novel's scaffolding. The second-pass counterfactual holds: fixing the listed findings would produce a manuscript with a credible shortlist case.
